www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 36 --

Welcome to the Product Success Formula!
My name is Rob Ward and I’m the co-founder
of Quad Lock. I’ve been developing smartphone
mounting technology for the past eight years. Since
then, I’ve bootstrapped Quad Lock from 0 to $9
million dollars in four years and have developed over
80 SKUs. Today we’ve sold 3 million units and ship
to over 100 countries.
In this module, I’ll teach you how to scale your
product line. That means leveraging your market
to its highest potential using different product
strategies.
The Product Success Formula
Product Success = Good Product + Good Market
+ Good Marketing/Operations
Before you jump in, it’s important to know that you
can optimize just about everything to get more sales.
It’s all about getting to know your customers a bit
better and strategically introducing product SKUs.
Let’s dive in!

MODULE INTRO

-- 3 of 36 --

Everything you’ve done by now is based on
assumption, in particular the way you think about
and communicate your product to your market.
Now that you’ve acquired a significant amount of
customers, it’s time to use their insights to optimize
your product and its marketing.
In this lesson, I’ll teach you to find and double down
on what actually makes your customers convert. This
is important because the market ultimately decides
on the success of a product. And here you’re getting
the market’s feedback before spending time and
money creating or changing your product.
Free Methods
• 	Read product reviews (Best & Worst)
• 	Google Trends
Paid Methods
• 	Landing page test
• 	Facebook Ads test
ACTION ITEMS
❏ 	Use these methods to validate your own
products.
❏ 	Write your findings in the workbook.

Product Reviews
Search your brand’s product reviews online by typing your
brand name + “product reviews.” Copy and paste or jot
down some reviews for the 3-4 start reviews. From there
hone in on the specific issue and make plans to fix it.

-- 5 of 36 --

Google trends
Go on Google Trends and write in the type of product
you’re interested in and/or have. For Quad Lock for
instance, it would be “iphone wireless charging.” Jot
down several of the keywords you wish to look into.
Then analyze how the product is trending over time. Pay
attention to which geographical market there is most
demand. If you see a steep positive increase in your
trend that means there’s a rapidly increasing demand
for it. If it’s even or in decline, it may be time to pivot to
another product.
Brand Key Terms:
Key Term 1:
Relationship: (Circle below)
Strong 	Positive
Weak 	Negative 	Flat

-- 6 of 36 --

Landing Page + Facebook Ads Tests
Create a landing page for a product you’re thinking of
launching with only a prototype. Open up the page for
pre-buyers and see how much demand you have for that
product. One of the best forms of validation is when there
are people willing to pay money for your product.
Checklist
❏ 	Create a prototype or even just a prototype design
❏ 	Gather photo/video assets for your landing page
❏ 	Draft the sales copy for the landing page
❏ 	Publish the landing page including the checkout/
payment options
❏ 	Set a benchmark for how many sales you have to get
to know when you’ve achieved validation
❏ 	Run the landing page as a Facebook ad and wait for
the sales
Jot down your totals for the following metrics below:
Click-Through Rates:
Cost Per Click:
Conversion Rate:
Return on Ad Spend:

-- 8 of 36 --

In this lesson, I’ll teach you the exact process I’ve
used to go about expanding Quad Lock to new
international markets. It’s all about determining
where in the world your brand is getting the most
searches and the cheapest customer acquisition
costs (ex. CPC, ROAS, etc.) This is another easy way
to get more customers without spending money on
developing a new product.
The 2-Step Market Validation Process
• 	Step 1: Find the market with the least resistance
• 	Feel the “market pull” (Google Trends)
• 	Cast a wide ad campaign and see where it
resonates
• 	Step 2: Consider all major factors
• 	Can we get the product there cheaply?
• 	Can we deliver the product there at an affordable
price?
• 	Can we maintain a good service that customers
will be happy with?
ACTION ITEMS:
❏ 	Validate a new market using the methods in this
lesson and write your results in the workbook.
“Sometimes the easiest growth is taking an existing
product and putting it in a new market.”
LESSON:
MARKET VALIDATION:

-- 12 of 36 --

In this lesson, I’ll show you how to create invaluable
customer surveys. The data you gather here will be
the lifeblood of your brand’s success. So it’s important
to tailor your marketing and product/s to the biggest
majority of your customers.
Self-Assessment Questions for Creating a Survey
• 	What is it that we don’t know about our customers?
• 	What assumptions have we been making until now?
• 	What’s an unbiased way we can ask our customers
questions?
• 	How can we get usable information that will
improve our marketing?
Survey Tips
• 	Don’t offer a reward or prize
• 	Set time expectations for completion
• 	Aim for good, clean data
• 	Send the survey at most once a year
ACTION ITEMS
❏ 	Draft a list of questions in the space provided in
the workbook.
❏ 	Send out your survey using Typeform, Survey
Monkey or Google Forms.
“The closer you can get to telling your customer what
they want to hear, the cheaper you can acquire that
customer.”

DATA

-- 15 of 36 --

In this lesson I’ll teach you my framework for making
sure your brand’s foundation is strong enough
to start adding onto its product offering. This is
important because you want to make sure that every
decision you make is pushing the business forward
and not shooting it in the foot.
Flagship/Onboarding Products
Your best, most pivotal products that bring in new
customers
The “Second Album” Framework
Four factors to consider before introducing a new
product:
1. 	Existing product mastery
2. 	Onboarding vs. Complementary product
3. 	Market & product validation
4. 	Finances
LESSON:
HOW TO KNOW WHEN TO
MAKE YOUR “2ND ALBUM”
MODULE:
PRODUCT
SUCCESS FORMULA
(CREATING NEW SKUS)
“With a new product comes more complexity.”
ACTION ITEMS
❏ 	Fill out the “Second Album” Framework exercise
in your workbook to see if you’re ready to
expand your product offering.
COURSE WORKBOOK
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 16 of 36 --

The “Second Album” Framework
Evaluate your business and product offering in the spaces provided.
1. 	Existing product mastery
❏ 	Are an overwhelming majority of your reviews in the 4 or 5-star range?
❏ 	Is there any way you could make your existing product better?
2. 	Onboarding vs. Complementary product
❏ 	Is your flagship product a leader in the market?
❏ 	Do you have lesser-priced products that complement your flagship/onboarding product?
3. 	Market & product validation
❏ 	Is there an increasing demand for your product on Google Trends?
❏ 	Are people willing to pre-buy your product?
❏ 	Have you exhausted all options for market expansion?
4. 	Finances
❏ 	How wide are your product margins?
❏ 	Would you consider your customer acquisition spend high or low or just right?
❏ 	Have you tightened your supply chain costs?
TOOLS & RESOURCES
• 	What You Should Know About Creating
a Second Product Line (American
Express)
• 	Here’s How to Not Be a One-Hit Wonder
(The Next Web)
COURSE WORKBOOK
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 18 of 36 --

In this lesson, I’ll teach you about the two different
avenues you can take when considering a new
product. It’s important to make these distinctions
because they will ultimately determine the number of
resources your brand will invest in the product.
“Me-Too” Product
A product that’s already been successfully
introduced by other companies
Pros
• 	Don’t have to build the market
• 	Works as a cross-sell or upsell
Cons
• 	Can’t grow the business off it
• 	Not going to own the market
Questions
• 	What is something we’re already doing that we
could leverage?
• 	Are other brands having success selling this
product?
• 	Is this a product that your customer needs/
wants? (Validate through survey)
• 	How can we sell this product better than
competitors?
“Break-the-Ceiling” Product
A new, innovative product that addresses an unsolved
consumer problem
Pros
• 	Can propel the business to a new level
• 	Can own the market
Cons
• 	Harder/more expensive to get off the ground
• 	Have to build the market
Questions
• 	What are growing markets? Are there any new pain
points that are coming up in these markets?
ACTION ITEM
❏ 	Decide whether your next product will be a “Me-
Too” product or a “Break-the-Ceiling” product, or a
mix.
LESSON:

In this lesson I”ll teach you how to approach
flagship/onboarding products. This type of product is
most likely what got you to where you are right now.
They’re simply your best, most pivotal products that
bring in new customers.
It’s important to understand the nature of this type of
product because everything you’ve done with your
existing product may not translate to your second
product. In other words, you’ll want to leverage
your existing business model but do away with your
approach to marketing.
ACTION ITEM
❏ 	If you’re pursuing a new flagship/onboarding
product, use the workbook to map out your
strategy.

PRODUCTS

-- 24 of 36 --

In this lesson, I’ll teach you about the two types of
complementary products: cross-sells and upsells.
Complementary products are additional products
that you try to sell to your customers, either after
they’ve made a purchase or during the checkout
process. And your new secret weapon.
The goal of having these products is to maximize
Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) by increasing the
Average Order Value (AOV). And the beautiful thing
about them is that if you strategize them correctly,
you’ll have more headroom and more money to
spend on acquiring customers.
Email Tips for Complementary Products
• 	Segment your list based on the customer survey
results
• 	Time your email based on the product’s
honeymoon phase
• 	Ensure a positive customer experience before
and after the email
This image shows the benefits of complementary products which include no customer acquisition costs or pick,
pack and shipping fees because they’ll be lumped in with the onboarding product sale.
LESSON:

PRODUCTS

In this lesson, I’ll teach you how to create a pricing
strategy that focuses on customer acquisition
costs, arguably the biggest focus area to scale your
product. This is important because as the following
images show, they can really go a long way.
Common Pricing Mistakes
• 	Underestimating CAC
• 	Focusing on (%) margin instead of ($) margin
• 	Starting with a high price and reducing it

-- 30 of 36 --

In this lesson, I’ll teach you how to determine
whether or not your brand should pursue distribution
or a mix of distribution and direct-to-consumer. This
is important because as a general rule of thumb
going through big retailers is no longer necessary -
or the best strategy. So in most cases it’s better to
prioritize direct-to-consumer and tack on distribution
where it makes strategic sense.
Reasons for Distribution
• 	Geographic location of the distributor increases
accessibility
• 	Distributor specializes in a niche market
• 	Distributor can adapt the product in ways your
can’t
LESSON:
D2C VS. DISTRIBUTION
MODULE:
PRODUCT
SUCCESS FORMULA
(CREATING NEW SKUS)
“Whoever owns the relationship with the customer is
going to be the winner.”
